edited.sourceText: Yeah, I'll try to see, like, what building, exactly where they're staying, that kind of location.

edited.speaker: Pablo
edited.text: Go ahead and roll. You can roll twice as well.
edited.sourceText: Okay, yeah, go ahead and roll. You can roll two as well.

edited.speaker: Pablo
edited.text: With a strong roll, you both overhear a couple of guards talking: "I can't believe Captain Togaloo just let them in." "Yeah, the captain knows what she's doing—and normally having pirates in your city would be a cause for concern." "They can't possibly do anything here. There's no sea nearby." "Yes, they're completely harmless." One adds, "I'm heading to Bwamandu."

I can't believe Captain Togaloo just let them in. Yeah, the captain knows what she's doing, and normally having pirates in your city would be a cause for concern. They can't possibly do anything here. There's no sea nearby. Yes, they're completely harmless. I'm going to go to Balmondu.

edited.speaker: Victoria
edited.text: Wait—how far away are we?
edited.sourceText: Wait, how far away are we?

edited.speaker: Pablo
edited.text: About a thirty-minute walk.
edited.sourceText: A 30-minute walk at that.

edited.speaker: Lisa
edited.text: We need to gather information on the way and slowly hone in on exactly where he is and what he's been doing.
edited.sourceText: Okay. We need some information on the way and slowly hone in on exactly where he's at and what he's been doing.

edited.speaker: Pablo
edited.text: You enter the Bwamandu district. As you walk through it, you notice a distinct lack of Charau-ka—they're all gone. Usually there are beggars asking for gold and scraps, a few doing odd jobs and errands, some drinking. Even though it's slums, it's where the poor Charau-ka live in little tents pitched against houses, and it smells foul. Now it feels strangely empty.
edited.sourceText: Yeah. But anyways, you get into the Guamandu district and as you're walking through it, you notice a distinct lack of charter call. Okay. They're all gone. There were beggars around begging you guys for gold and scraps. There was a couple doing odd work and errands, some drinking. You know, just in general, Balmondu, even though it was like slums, Like... like, you know, it's where all the poor beggars live and stuff, and just live in like little tents pitched up against houses, and it smells gross and disgusting. How does it just seem like the charmed car? It's kind of weird.

edited.speaker: Lisa
edited.text: What about the business district?
edited.sourceText: How about the, like, business owner?

edited.speaker: Pablo
edited.text: The business district is the same—distinct lack of Charau-ka. You might see one or two passed out, very incapacitated.
edited.sourceText: The business center, same thing. Distinct lack of Charu Kha. Yeah. You might see one or two passed out, very incapacitated.

edited.speaker: Lisa
edited.text: But Fister would be nowhere to be seen?
edited.sourceText: But Pfister would be nowhere to be seen?

edited.speaker: Pablo
edited.text: Fister's Bar is at the end of the Bwamandu district, so you're heading in the right direction. His place is furthest south, closest to the wall where the sewer tunnel is—already on the outskirts. That makes sense: the Charau-ka get pushed to the worst parts of Bwamandu, where there's little infrastructure and sewage collects. It's gross.

edited.speaker: Lisa
edited.text: So we're following the smell.

edited.speaker: Pablo
edited.text: At the center of the crowd you see a tall, lanky human man—about six foot five—with ginger hair cut a little long. A large rifle is strung across his back in a leather cover. It's high-tech weaponry; you recognize it as a firearm, though the Charau-ka probably don't. Standing beside him is a small orange-haired charau-ka monkey with golden-tamarin vibes, shouting in demonic Charau-ka language. He wears a little pirate hat with a feather, and next to him sits another captain's hat with two big white feathers matching the monkey's smaller hat. The monkey delivers passionate speeches about riches and adventure—how Charau-ka are strong conquerors who should follow other strong conquerors, and how they're rotting away here in the city.
edited.sourceText: Okay, this is what you guys see. Now, obviously, he's not pointing his gun. He has a large, long rifle strung across his back in leather cover to protect it from the elements. It's very high-tech weaponry for what you see. You only recognize it because you're all traveled individuals, but these Charu Kha probably don't know what it is. edited.speaker: Pablo
edited.text: Mid-speech, he turns to three Black Heart Corsairs behind him—no-nonsense, each a little taller than him in full plate with giant pikes. Two wear helmets; the leader does not, scanning the crowd seriously while holding his pike at the ready. All three wear gray sashes repeating the motto: we take what we want. He pauses; the monkey pauses; the Charau-ka listen with bated breath. He says, "I took what I wanted, and I want you to have it." The monkey and two guards pull sacks from behind them. The monkey drags one out, opens it, and copper pieces spill into the crowd—some specked with blood. One Charau-ka pockets a coin and a mad frenzy erupts as they fight over the copper. Everyone roll Acrobatics or Athletics—whatever you prefer to fight through the chaos.
